fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that filename parsing library we've been talking about for the Kavita cataloguing backend. Basically the idea is that users are constantly complaining that their manga and comic collections show up in completely random order, and titles look like garbage because of all the bracket noise and underscores from various scanlation groups. We want a clean library that can look at a raw filename and spit out structured info — stuff like the title, which volume or chapter it is, whether it's some kind of special release, what format the file is, etc. It should work for both manga-style naming (the chapter-heavy stuff with all the weird group tags) and western comic naming (the Asterix/Batman style with year parentheticals and publisher info). Also needs to handle sorting — you know how file explorers sort x10 before x3, we need the human-friendly version of that. Should be pure functions, no database, no network. I want this modular, not one giant file. Refer to the same sentinel pattern we used in the chapter-numbering edge cases from that old sorting module — same idea applies here for volumes too. Also make sure the special-release detection covers the usual suspects that the ops team flagged last quarter. Language should be Python. One entry point script that reads JSON from stdin and routes to the right function.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for the missing-number behavior, we should be totally consistent here. When no volume marker is present the function must return the string '-100000' and that applies to both manga volume parsing and comic volume parsing. Same deal for chapter/issue parsing: when no chapter or issue marker is present the function must return the string '-100000' for both manga chapter and comic issue extractors. Don’t swap in None, an empty string, or 0. This is the same sentinel return value used in both parse_volume and parse_chapter when the marker is absent, and it needs to stay aligned across src/parsers/manga_parser.py and src/parsers/comic_parser.py.

A couple more specifics on file handling too. A file counts as a cover image when its base name without the extension matches things like 'cover', 'folder', '!cover', or a volume cover pattern, and only if it’s a supported image extension. Back-cover filenames should not match, and dot-prefixed files should be excluded. For junk path filtering, the blacklist check is not just for the last path piece — it applies to every segment in the path, including stuff like __MACOSX, recycle bin variants, snapshot folders, and similar system/junk directories.

For normalization, the key should come from lower-casing the input and then stripping spaces, underscores, hyphens and dashes, plus most punctuation, while still preserving meaningful characters and non-Latin scripts like Cyrillic, CJK, and Korean. End result should just be one continuous lowercase string with no separators. For the zero-padding helper, it should left-pad a numeric string to width 3 using '0', but leave anything already at 3 or more chars alone. So input '1' → '001', input '10' → '010', input '100' → '100'.

On the little utility side, the font-src separator needs to split a CSS font-source declaration into exactly three parts, each printed on its own line: first the opening directive through the opening quote or parenthesis, then the file path, then the closing quote or parenthesis. Example stays exactly 'src: url(fonts/file.ttf)' → 'src: url(\n' + 'fonts/file.ttf\n' + ')\n'. The range helpers should accept either a plain number string or a hyphen-separated pair. min_from_range returns the lower bound as a number, max_from_range returns the upper bound, and if it’s just one number then both return that same number. If the input can’t be parsed, return 0 rather than raising anything.

Last piece is the adapter shape. The execution adapter should read a JSON object from stdin with at least a 'feature' key, like 'feature1_1', and an 'input' key, route that to the right domain function, and print the result plus a trailing newline to stdout. The core parsing/domain functions themselves should stay clean and not depend directly on stdin/stdout.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def main() -> None  # stdin JSON router dispatching to domain functions across src/ modules

trigger_keywords: half chapter handling
chapter ending in b
trailing b in chapter number
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A chapter designator that ends with the letter 'b' (e.g. '15b') represents a half-chapter and must be mapped to a .5 decimal (e.g. '15.5'). Leading zeroes are also stripped from the numeric part.

trigger_keywords: volume range format
hyphenated volume span
multiple volumes in one file
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a volume marker covers a hyphenated span (e.g. 'Vol.1-3'), return the span verbatim after normalizing leading zeroes on each bound (e.g. '1-3'). Do not collapse it to a single number.

trigger_keywords: natural sort order
human order for filenames
numeric segments in sort
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Natural/logical sort must split strings into alternating text and digit runs and compare digit runs numerically, not lexically. Non-alphanumeric-leading entries sort before alphanumeric ones. Letter comparison is case-insensitive. This applies to both the string logical sort and the numeric filename sort features.

trigger_keywords: chapter sort sentinel last
sort -100000 at end
unnumbered chapter ordering
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the 'sort chapters with default last' feature, the sentinel value -100000 must always appear at the end of the sorted list regardless of its numeric value. In the 'sort chapters with default first' variant, a sentinel value of 0 must appear before all real positive chapter numbers.

trigger_keywords: special release keywords manga
omake one-shot detection
what counts as manga special
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A manga name is classified as a special when it contains keywords including but not limited to: Special, OneShot, One-Shot, Omake, Extra, Side Story, Bonus, Annual, Prologue. The check is case-insensitive and should also fire on the short SP<digits> marker.

trigger_keywords: special release keywords comic
TPB annual bonus detection
what counts as comic special
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A comic name is classified as a special when it contains keywords including but not limited to: Annual, One-Shot, TPB, Omnibus, Bonus, Deluxe Edition, Hors-Serie, HS, Rebirth Deluxe. The check is case-insensitive.

trigger_keywords: edition label extraction
omnibus uncensored edition parse
what editions are recognized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The edition extractor looks for standalone edition keywords directly in the filename such as 'Omnibus', 'Uncensored', 'Director's Cut', etc. Curly-brace wrapped phrases like '{Full Contact Edition}' do NOT count and should return an empty string.

trigger_keywords: media format classification
file extension to format category
archive image ebook mapping
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Extensions map to coarse categories as follows — Archive: cbz, zip, rar, cbr, 7z, cbt; Image: png, jpg, jpeg, webp, gif, avif; EBook: epub; PDF/page-description: pdf; anything else returns 'Unknown'. Output is the category name with initial capital (e.g. 'Image', 'Archive', 'Unknown').

trigger_keywords: volume keywords multiple languages
french tome hors serie volume
cyrillic volume keyword support
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Volume extraction must recognize keywords across multiple languages: English (Vol, Volume, v), French (Tome, T, HS, Hors-Serie), and should handle Cyrillic naming patterns. The matching is case-insensitive and handles optional punctuation like periods after the keyword.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the special-release detection covers the usual suspects that the ops team flagged last quarter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The keyword lists for is_manga_special (OneShot, Omake, Extra, Special, Side Story, Bonus, Annual, Prologue, SP<digits>) and is_comic_special (Annual, One-Shot, TPB, Omnibus, Bonus, Deluxe Edition, Hors-Serie, HS, Rebirth) as exercised in feature2_3 and feature2_4 test cases

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify off the top of my head — you'll want to look at the public test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference with the feature descriptions in start.md. The test cases are the authoritative contract for exact input/output behaviour.

feature1_1__parse_series.json.description: Given a media file or folder name, extract the human-readable title of the work, stripping volume/chapter markers, bracketed scanlation/release tags, edition tags, parenthetical year/metadata, file extensions, and underscores. Names that contain only a volume or chapter token with no title yield an empty title. The function operates uniformly across Latin, Cyrillic, CJK and Korean names.

cases.input: Killing Bites Vol. 0001 Ch. 0001 - Galactica Scanlations (gb)
My Girlfriend Is Shobitch v01 - ch. 09 - pg. 008.png

feature1_2__parse_volume.json.description: Given a media file or folder name, extract the volume designator as a string. A single volume yields its bare number; a hyphenated span yields the span verbatim; decimal volumes are preserved. When no volume marker is present the function returns the loose-leaf sentinel string (-100000). Supports volume keywords across multiple languages.

cases.input: Killing Bites Vol. 0001 Ch. 0001 - Galactica Scanlations (gb)
My Girlfriend Is Shobitch v01 - ch. 09 - pg. 008.png

feature1_3__parse_chapter.json.description: Given a media file or folder name, extract the chapter designator as a string. A single chapter yields its number with leading zeroes removed; a hyphenated span yields the normalized span; half-chapters (e.g. a trailing 'b') map to a .5 decimal. When no chapter marker is present the function returns the default-chapter sentinel string (-100000).

cases.input: Killing Bites Vol. 0001 Ch. 0001 - Galactica Scanlations (gb)
My Girlfriend Is Shobitch v01 - ch. 09 - pg. 008.png

feature1_4__parse_comic_series.json.description: Given a comic file or folder name, extract the series title, removing issue numbers, volume/tome markers, parenthetical years and publisher/scanlation tags. Handles English, French (Tome/HS) and Cyrillic naming patterns.

cases.input: 04 - Asterix the Gladiator (1964) (Digital-Empire) (WebP by Doc MaKS)
The First Asterix Frieze (WebP by Doc MaKS)

feature1_5__parse_comic_volume.json.description: Given a comic file or folder name, extract the volume/tome designator as a string. Recognizes 'v', 'Vol', 'V', 'T', 'Tome' style markers (including decimals). When no volume is present the function returns the loose-leaf sentinel string (-100000).

cases.input: 04 - Asterix the Gladiator (1964) (Digital-Empire) (WebP by Doc MaKS)

feature1_6__parse_comic_chapter.json.description: Given a comic file or folder name, extract the issue/chapter number as a string (decimals preserved). When no issue number can be found the function returns the default-chapter sentinel string (-100000).

cases.input: 04 - Asterix the Gladiator (1964) (Digital-Empire) (WebP by Doc MaKS)

feature1_7__parse_edition.json.description: Given a file name, extract any edition label it advertises (for example an omnibus or uncensored marking), returning the matched edition text or an empty string when none is present.

feature1_8__parse_format.json.description: Classify a file by its extension into a coarse media format: an archive, an image, an electronic book, a page-description document, or unknown when the extension is unrecognized. Output is the format name.

feature2_1__has_special_marker.json.description: Report whether a name carries the short special-issue marker (an 'SP' immediately followed by digits). Returns a boolean.

feature2_2__parse_special_index.json.description: Return the numeric index encoded by the short special-issue marker ('SP' followed by digits), or 0 when the marker is absent.

feature2_3__is_manga_special.json.description: Decide whether a manga file name denotes a special/extra/omake/one-shot rather than a regular numbered release. Returns a boolean.

cases.input: Beelzebub Special OneShot - Minna no Kochikame x Beelzebub (2016) [Mangastream].cbz

feature2_4__is_comic_special.json.description: Decide whether a comic file name denotes a special (annual, one-shot, TPB, bonus, omnibus, hors-serie, etc.) rather than a regular numbered issue. Returns a boolean.

cases.input: Batman - Detective Comics - Rebirth Deluxe Edition Book 02 (2018) (digital) (Son of Ultron-Empire)

feature2_5__clean_special_title.json.description: Strip the short special-issue marker from a title and normalize underscores/whitespace, returning the cleaned title.

feature3_1__clean_title.json.description: Clean a release title for display: replace underscores with spaces, drop leading release-group brackets and stray leading dashes, remove trailing parenthetical metadata, and (when flagged as a comic) drop a trailing edition phrase. Input carries the raw title and a comic flag.

feature3_2__clean_author.json.description: Trim surrounding whitespace from an author name while preserving internal punctuation and ordering.

feature3_3__normalize.json.description: Produce a canonical comparison key for a name by lower-casing, removing spaces, underscores and most punctuation while preserving certain meaningful characters and non-Latin scripts.

feature3_4__remove_leading_zeroes.json.description: Remove leading zeroes from a numeric string, leaving the significant digits (and at least a single digit).

feature3_5__pad_zeros.json.description: Left-pad a numeric string with zeroes to a minimum width of three characters; strings already that wide are unchanged.

feature4_1__is_archive.json.description: Report whether a file name has an archive extension (e.g. cbz/zip/rar/cbr/7z/cbt). Returns a boolean.

feature4_2__is_book.json.description: Report whether a file name has a book extension (epub or pdf). Returns a boolean.

feature4_3__is_epub.json.description: Report whether a file name has the epub extension (case-insensitive). Returns a boolean.

feature4_4__is_image.json.description: Report whether a file name is a supported raster image (png/jpg/jpeg/webp/gif/avif), excluding dot-prefixed hidden files. Returns a boolean.

feature4_5__is_cover_image.json.description: Report whether a path's file name denotes a front cover image (e.g. cover/folder/!cover or a volume cover), but not a back cover. Returns a boolean.

feature5_1__normalize_path.json.description: Normalize a filesystem path by converting backslashes to forward slashes and collapsing duplicate separators.

feature5_2__has_blacklisted_folder.json.description: Report whether any path segment is a system/junk folder that should be skipped (e.g. macOS metadata, recycle/snapshot/trash folders). Returns a boolean.

feature6_1__min_from_range.json.description: Given a number or a hyphenated range, return the minimum value as a number. Pure numbers return themselves; ranges return the lower bound; unparseable inputs return 0.

feature6_2__max_from_range.json.description: Given a number or a hyphenated range, return the maximum value as a number. Pure numbers return themselves; ranges return the upper bound; unparseable inputs return 0.

feature7_1__balanced_paren.json.description: Report whether a string's parentheses are correctly balanced and properly nested across the entire string. Returns a boolean.

cases.input: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog
(The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog)

feature7_2__balanced_bracket.json.description: Report whether a string's square brackets are correctly balanced and properly nested across the entire string. Returns a boolean.

cases.input: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog
[The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog]

feature8_1__font_src_match.json.description: Report whether a CSS font source declaration references an external font file via url(...) or local(...), excluding inline data: URIs. Returns a boolean.

feature8_2__font_src_separate.json.description: Split a CSS font source declaration into its three parts: the opening directive up to the opening quote/paren, the referenced file path, and the closing quote/paren. Output is the three parts, one per line, in order.

feature9_1__string_logical_sort.json.description: Sort a list of strings in natural (human) order: embedded digit runs compare numerically rather than lexically, letters compare case-insensitively, and non-alphanumeric-leading entries sort ahead of alphanumeric ones. Output is the sorted list, one entry per line.

feature9_2__numeric_sort.json.description: Sort a list of file names using the same natural-order rule, so numeric segments order numerically. Output is the sorted list, one entry per line.

feature9_3__chapter_sort_default_last.js.description: Sort a list of chapter numbers ascending, but always place the default/unnumbered sentinel value (-100000) at the end. Output is the sorted sequence, one number per line.

feature9_4__chapter_sort_default_first.j.description: Sort a list of chapter numbers in ascending order; a special default/unnumbered sentinel value, when present, is ordered before all real numbers. Output is the sorted sequence, one number per line.
